articles.title: Perlin's Noise Algorithm
articles.summary: Perlin's noise algorithm is a fundamental concept in computer graphics and game development. Developed by Ken Perlin in 1983, this procedural texture generation technique uses mathematical formulas to create natural-looking patterns. The algorithm has been widely adopted in various fields due to its ability to generate realistic textures with varying levels of detail and complexity. As developers continue to push the boundaries of what is possible in graphics and game development, understanding Perlin's noise algorithm remains crucial for creating immersive and engaging experiences.

articles.title: Ghost Cut – or why Cut and Paste is broken everywhere
articles.summary: Cut & Paste is broken in every word processor, code editor, and browser text area you've ever used due to its undo and reflow issues. A cut can't be fully undone, the document reflows after a cut, and even though a cut and paste are conceptually atomic, they require undoing intermediate edits before restoring the original state. The developer has introduced "Ghost Cut" which fixes these flaws by fading selected text, making it inert, and allowing for a single atomic move operation without the clipboard involved.

articles.title: Why USB-B never became as popular as USB-A or USB-C
articles.summary: The USB-B connector was never meant to replace USB-A or USB-C due to its design and intended use as a peripheral connection point. Its squarish shape and broader size made it unsuitable for portable electronics, while the reversible nature of USB-C provided a more convenient and secure option. Despite being three decades old, USB-B remains in use primarily due to compatibility concerns with existing cables and workflows.

articles.title: SIMD for Collision
articles.summary: In Box3D's collision solver, a wide SIMD approach is used to process multiple work units at the same time, solving four contact points simultaneously. This is different from narrow SIMD where a 3-vector is processed in a SIMD register and then vector math is applied as SIMD intrinsics. The performance difference between these two approaches may not be obvious due to the convex pile benchmark's unique use of boxes and boulders, which can greatly impact performance.

The introduction of this flag has been met with criticism, particularly regarding its potential misuse as an attack vector. The development of git rev-parse only as a subcommand and the addition of --end-of-options as a standalone flag have raised concerns about argument injection vulnerabilities.
